Continuously, we are planning for further enhancements and additional features in the future, such as Delta Sharing support and access to external object storages. Please visit our SAP Road Map Explorer to stay tuned for the upcoming updates.

entry.title: Connecting the Facts: SAP HANA Cloud’s Knowledge Graph Engine for Business Context
entry.content:
Introduction:

In Q1 2025, SAP is planning to release the knowledge graph engine as a new capability for SAP HANA Cloud platform. This new multi-model engine allows businesses to connect and analyze information more effectively. SAP HANA Cloud will enable smarter applications and improve user experiences by using data stored in a graph to answer complex questions.

Knowledge graphs connect corporate knowledge, which makes them especially beneficial for powering large language models (LLMs) and generative AI capabilities. Developers can use business context to create applications that provide more precise and relevant answers, enhancing user interactions and improving business processes.

For many, the topic of knowledge graphs is new and exciting. I share below a brief introduction on the major concepts and how SAP plans for those concepts to materialize in SAP HANA Cloud.

Ontologies

An ontology is a guiding framework used to define at a higher level (i.e. classes, categories, groupings, etc.) the facts that occur in a domain and how those higher-level descriptions should relate to each other. Instead of focusing on the facts, an ontology regulates the resulting knowledge graph. For example, in the context of movies, an ontology could define the concept of "movies" and its attributes like title, director, genre, and release date, making the framework flexible and scalable for future data. Ontologies act as the blueprint for knowledge graphs, structuring and connecting data to allow deeper insights and reasoning.

Ontologies are critical for certain use cases where deep, structured relationships are required. For example, semantic networks often represent ontologies to map out concepts and relationships, and these structures can help with reasoning and inference. However, not every knowledge graph requires a complex ontology from the start. As needs grow, ontologies can be added to enrich the graph. The flexibility of knowledge graphs allows one to start small, deliver value quickly and expand over time as use cases become more complex.

Movies Ontology

Knowledge Graph

A knowledge graph is a structured representation of data (adhering to ontology rules), enhanced by real-world information. The knowledge graph captures relationships between entities, such as people, products, or events, and connects them in a meaningful way. Unlike data in normalized relations, a knowledge graph organizes information as triples where facts are represented as subject, predicate, and object. These triples form the core of the knowledge graph, revealing the logical relationships between different entities.

For example, consider the above example of movies ontology. A knowledge graph can store the facts of the movie Jurassic Park into a tripes format: Jurassic Park (subject) is directed by (predicate) Steven Spielberg (object). When you add more details, like the year of release, production company, and the sequel information, you can see the formation of a knowledge graph that connects all these data points.

 

Knowledge graphs are set apart by their ability to traverse relationships, much like navigating through a web of interconnected facts. Like reading through an encyclopedia, each piece of knowledge leads to another, revealing a chain of actionable insights. This search ability is especially powerful for tasks requiring complex reasoning, such as analyzing a supply chain. A virtual supply chain representation is a great example of connecting independent data silos to create knowledge. A company can trace the origin of materials, understand how external factors like weather disruptions in a supplier's region might impact production, and identify alternative suppliers with more sustainable practices. Thus, the knowledge graphs can help businesses mitigate potential risks and optimize decision-making in real time.

Movies Knowledge Graph

SAP HANA Cloud Knowledge Graph Engine Planned Features

With the upcoming knowledge graph engine, SAP HANA Cloud gains the ability to manage and query semantically connected relationships – all within the same database as other relational and multi-model engines.

The key highlights are:

  1. Native RDF Triple Store: One of the key features of the SAP HANA Cloud knowledge graph engine is its native support for RDF triple store. RDF (Resource Description Framework) is a widely accepted standard for modeling and representing data in graph structures. With this support, the platform allows users to store and manage ‘subject-predicate-object’ triples directly, providing an efficient and scalable way to represent relationships between data entities. This native capability ensures that the data stored in the knowledge graph adheres to global standards for graph databases, enabling seamless and deeper insights from complex relationships.
  2. SQL and SPARQL Interoperability: The knowledge graph engine will support SPARQL, the specialized query language for knowledge graph data, while tightly integrating it with SQL. This is achieved with the introduction of the following new SQL constructs:
    • SPARQL_TABLE (): A SQL table function that allows developers to embed SPARQL SELECT queries within SQL statements, returning results in a table format that can be processed further using SQL operations like filters, joins, and aggregations.  
    • SPARQL_EXECUTE (): A Stored procedure that provides an interface for executing all types of SPARQL statements, including both SPARQL Query Forms and SPARQL Update.
    • SQL_TABLE (): A SPARQL function that allows users to embed SQL queries directly within SPARQL statements. This function federates queries to the SQL engine (HANA index server process), with the results of the SQL statement being converted into graph patterns. SQL projections are mapped to corresponding SPARQL variables, enabling smooth integration between SQL and graph data.

SAP HANA Cloud knowledge graph engine vs the SAP HANA Cloud graph engine

SAP HANA Cloud already offers a graph engine designed to handle property graphs, which are excellent for analyzing relationships between entities. Now let’s use a retail supply chain use case as an example to highlight the differences between property graphs and knowledge graphs.

Property Graphs: These graphs are ideal for analyzing relationships between entities. In a property graph, data is represented as nodes (entities) and edges (relationships), where each edge can hold different properties that describe the connection between nodes. For instance, in a retail scenario, a property graph might show that Supplier A "delivers" Product X to Store Y. Another edge might indicate that Supplier B also delivers Product X to Store Z. This structure focuses on the relationships between entities, making it easy to map out who supplies what to whom, and where the products are sold.

Knowledge Graphs: In contrast, knowledge graphs focus on establishing facts and the logical relationships between those facts. They use a subject-predicate-object structure (triples), where each fact is stored as a relationship. For example, in a supply chain context, a knowledge graph captures facts such as "Product A is made from recycled materials," "Supplier X follows sustainable sourcing practices," and "Store Y is located in an eco-friendly area." This type of graph goes beyond just showing who supplies the product, it allows you to ask more sophisticated questions, like "Which suppliers with eco-friendly practices are providing products to stores located in green-certified regions?"

In short, while property graphs help you visualize and analyze relationships between different entities, knowledge graphs bring context and reasoning into the mix, making it easier to derive actionable insights. Together, both graphs give you a complete picture of how entities interact and the deeper implications of those relationships.

Use Cases:

  1. Enhance your Intelligent Data Applications & Gen AI:  The in-memory database capabilities of SAP HANA Cloud already provide high-performance data processing, but the addition of a knowledge graph enables applications to gain an extra layer of understanding. This new feature is particularly valuable when it comes to enabling Gen AI use cases, such as grounding large language models (also called GraphRAG) in domain-specific knowledge. By storing and retrieving semantic facts about a business's operations, relationships, and data, the knowledge graph can provide LLMs with the context they need to deliver more accurate and relevant responses - ultimately improving the quality of human-computer interaction.
  2. Establish a Data Fabric: With the Knowledge Graph Engine working seamlessly alongside the other multi-model engines in SAP HANA Cloud, such as the relational data, spatial, graph, JSON document store and vector engines, businesses can now bring structured and unstructured data together in a single platform, establishing a data fabric. This unified approach allows companies to query and analyze data holistically, breaking down traditional data silos. The semantic linking of data creates a unified view, allowing for accurate contextual understanding of complex concepts, such as the term "material" in different scenarios like manufacturing, sales, or procurement.
  3. Improved Decision Making & Logical Inference: Knowledge graphs in general contribute to better data governance by establishing clearer relationships and rules for data entities, which improves logical reasoning and helps users make more informed, accurate decisions with fewer errors.

Conclusion:

With the addition of such advanced capabilities, SAP HANA Cloud continues to be the database of choice for developers and organizations, supporting critical applications within the SAP Business Technology Platform ecosystem. To stay tuned for further updates in this area, follow me on the SAP Community for regular insights, and check out Thomas Hammer’s What's New in SAP HANA Cloud blog series for the latest developments.

entry.title: A Sneak Peek into the Upcoming Dynamic Elasticity (Elastic Compute Node) in SAP HANA Cloud
entry.content:
Already featured on the SAP Road Map Explorer and announced at SAP TechEd, the upcoming SAP HANA Cloud, SAP HANA database QRC 04/2024 release will introduce Elastic Compute Node (ECN), a new dynamic elasticity feature.

As the name indicates, ECN is a read-only compute node (computeserver) that can be easily provisioned and deprovisioned based on incoming workloads or customer demands. With ECN, customers can efficiently address expected peak workloads, such as heavy planning jobs and Black Friday, while optimizing the size of the SAP HANA database in SAP HANA Cloud based on average workloads because ECNs can be spun up only when needed and then removed.

ECN has already been successfully used by SAP Integrated Business Planning (IBP) and SAP Datasphere for the purpose of addressing peak workloads while optimizing TCO, and now, it will be available for SAP HANA Cloud standalone use cases from QRC 04/2024. If you want to know more about how IBP benefits from the gained elasticity, then have a look at this blogpost and learn about IBP’s journey to SAP HANA Cloud.

Please find a nutshell of this new capability below:

  • A read-only compute node where the workload can be distributed.
  • Guided elasticity via REST API, btp Command Line Interface (CLI), or cf (Cloud Foundry) CLI.
  • Basically, equivalent to the indexserver but no persistency to store data permanently.
  • Replica tables and temporary tables can be created and stored in ECNs to accelerate query performance. These tables are removed when the ECNs are deprovisioned.
  • Since ECNs are not included in High Availability (HA) or Disaster Recovery (DR), this means there are no replicas for ECNs themselves for the purpose of HA/DR, resulting in a smaller footprint and lower TCO.
  • Workload Class-based dynamic workload routing.

As illustrated in the diagram above, while a 16 vCPUs and 256 GB SAP HANA database is intended for average workloads, a provision of 8 vCPUs and 128 GB ECN can be made on-demand to address peak workloads and then deprovisioned as needed. Essentially, the size of the ECN can be larger than the main SAP HANA database in SAP HANA Cloud, and multiple ECNs can also be provisioned. However, detailed restrictions, such as the maximum size of the ECN and the maximum number of parallel ECNs, will be announced with QRC 04/2024.

In conclusion, the upcoming introduction of ECN in SAP HANA Cloud represents a significant step forward in dynamic elasticity. This new feature will allow for greater flexibility and efficiency in addressing peak workloads, offering a promising solution for optimizing the size of the SAP HANA database while enhancing overall performance. As this is just a sneak peek and SAP HANA Cloud is continuously evolving, we encourage you to stay tuned for the upcoming updates.

entry.title: The next step towards cost-effectiveness and scalability with SAP HANA Cloud Multitenancy
entry.content:
With the upcoming release at the end of 2024, SAP HANA Cloud will introduce an innovation that supports native multitenancy capabilities on database-level. It allows you to manage data for different customers in dedicated database tenants sharing the same SAP HANA Cloud instance. 

Before we get into the details of the new feature, let’s look at multitenancy, what it is and why it matters.

Introduction

Multitenancy is a common architecture pattern in cloud environments, where multiple clients share the same resources and services. Each client is also named as tenant. While sharing the same resources, tenants are independent and separated from each other. You can think of an apartment house where the apartments have their own keys and own interiors but using the same infrastructure, e.g. for water and power supply. This “multitenant model” is very cost-effective as the individual units do not need to build their own expensive infrastructure.

Figure 1 - Multitenant Application

In the context of cloud applications, one multitenant application serves multiple customers in a single software instance. These customers are mapped to “application tenants”, sharing the same computing resources and services of the application. Finally, the data of the individual application tenants must be stored in an underlying database layer in “database tenants” (see Figure 1).

Why does Multitenancy matter?

With the multitenancy approach a cloud application uses a lower footprint of computing resources, as you don’t need to provision a dedicated software instance per customer. The cloud application can also simply scale by adding additional application tenants for new onboarded customers. With the rise of SaaS applications, the multitenant architecture becomes very popular, due to the significantly lower TCO. However, the application layer cannot provide enterprise qualities like customer-specific encryption or tenant-level data recovery. Those qualities can only be added by the database layer that ensures the logical separation of customer data, even though they are physically integrated in the same instance and sharing the same computing resources. 

The new SAP HANA Cloud Multitenancy capability will provide a modern data management layer for database tenants integrated in one database instance while keeping the balance between resource sharing and tenant separation. 

In the next section we will clarify some fundamental principles of SAP HANA Cloud database tenants.

What is a HANA Cloud database tenant?  

The SAP HANA Cloud database tenant is a first-class catalog object in the database that is associated with one or multiple database schemas. In other words, SAP HANA Cloud Multitenancy supports a schema-level multitenant model, where multiple database tenants are sharing the same SAP HANA Cloud instance and the same index server database process. This multitenant model provides a very scalable and cost-effective approach, starting from small up to large tenant sizes.

Since SAP HANA Cloud is a persistency service in the SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP), not only the SAP HANA Cloud database, but also each SAP HANA Cloud database tenant in the database is exposed as service instance to the SaaS multitenant applications. Whenever the application wants to persist data in an SAP HANA Cloud database tenant, it initially needs to create a database tenant as service instance and bind the corresponding application tenant or application service to the database tenant.

The SAP HANA Cloud database tenant itself is associated with one or multiple HDI-Containers/Schemas (see Figure 2). Both HDI-Containers and Schemas are also managed as service instances in BTP and can be added to or deleted from a database tenant at any time. 

Figure 2 - Database Tenant

To create database artefacts in HDI-Containers in a database tenant, you will use the Business Application Studio (BAS) that relies on HDI-Deployment. The BAS will connect directly to the HDI-Containers, as the database tenant association is transparent to it. For schemas, the application needs to create database artefacts via SQL-statements by using the database user and credentials of the schema.

How are SAP HANA Cloud database tenants managed?

As you can see in the center of Figure 3, there is a central Tenant Management Service (TMS) in BTP that provides all database tenant operations as REST APIs to the SaaS multitenant applications. The central TMS ensures that all HDI-Containers and schemas in a database tenant are handled consistently for tenant lifecycle operations.

In future, these TMS REST APIs can be consumed as CAP multitenancy services being part of the MTX package (MTX stands for multitenancy, feature toggle, and extensibility).

Figure 3 - HANA Cloud Database Tenants in the Business Technology Platform

Let’s look at the characteristics and planned features for SAP HANA Cloud database tenants in the next section.

Characteristics and Planned Features
  1. Tenant Encryption
    Each database tenant is encrypted with its own customer key by default. The customer key can be either managed by SAP (customer-specific encryption key) or managed by the customer (customer-managed key).

  2. Tenant Recovery
    You can recover each database tenant individually to any given point in time (PITR) within the defined backup retention time of the HANA Cloud instance.

  3. Tenant Move
    Database tenants can be moved between SAP HANA Cloud instances within the same datacenters. The feature will evolve towards an online tenant move (with near zero downtime) within and across datacenters. The feature will allow you e.g. to re-balance the number of managed tenants between SAP HANA Cloud instances or to relocate customer data.

  4. Tenant Data Copy
    To setup SAP HANA Cloudinstances with database tenants for test, training, or demo purposes, you can use the Tenant Data Copy feature. It allows you to copy tenant data between SAP HANA Cloudinstances. In its final version, the feature is also planned to allow a tenant copy across datacenters.

  5. Tenant Workload Management
    This feature avoids the so-called “noisy neighbor” effect, where database tenants may tend to claim computing resources as much as they need while other database tenants are suffering from lacking those resources. With tenant workload management you can assign workload classes with defined resource consumption limits to database tenants.

  6. Tenant Administration
    In the SAP HANA Cloud Central tooling you will get UI-based support to create, delete, recover database tenants, add, or remove HDI-Containers/schemas to database tenants as well as to monitor critical metrics of database tenants. Future development will include automation and mass-administration for hundreds or thousands of database tenants.

  7. Tenant Migration
    Coming from a HDI-Container or schema approach for multitenancy without isolation and recovery, cloud applications will migrate them to directly to database tenants leveraging all the new innovative features that are provided by SAP HANA Cloud.

Please note that not all of the planned features for SAP HANA Cloud Multitenancy will be available with the initial GA release by end of 2024. It is planned to deliver them gradually. Please follow the release information for multitenancy in the SAP HANA Cloud online help "What's New" section.

Conclusion

You can now benefit from the cost-effectiveness of SAP HANA Cloud Multitenancy, serving multiple customers while keeping their data logically separated within the same SAP HANA Cloud instance. Customers’ data is persisted in database tenants and encrypted with customer-specific keys. You can recover database tenants individually, without affecting other customers. You can scale your SAP HANA Cloud instance easily, starting with small database tenant sizes in a kilobyte range and growing as customer data requires. You can dynamically onboard new customers by adding new database tenants to the SAP HANA Cloud instance. And finally, you may use capabilities to gain fine-granular resource control and workload isolation for database tenants.

entry.title: Configure an SAP CAP Application to Connect to an SAP HANA Cloud Database in Another Global Account
entry.content:
How to Configure an SAP CAP Application to Connect to an SAP HANA Cloud Database in Another Global Account or Subaccount Without HDI

When your SAP HANA Cloud database is in another Global Account or subaccount, which prevents the natural use of the HDI container for database object management, you need to follow the steps below to configure your SAP CAP application and achieve a successful connection.

1: Configure a User-Provided Service

It is essential to create a user-provided service using the Cloud Foundry command line. This is important because SAP CAP requires the service to have three specific tags to automatically determine which configuration to use.

Run the following command:

cf create-user-provided-service ups-database-external -t "hana,database,relational"
  • ups-database-external: This is the name you assign to your service.
  • -t "hana,database,relational": These are the tags that CAP uses to identify the database service.

2: Modify the Service's JSON in SAP BTP

After creating the service via the command line, you need to specify the connection data in the service's JSON in SAP BTP.

The JSON should contain the following:

{
  "encrypt": true,
  "host": "your-hana-cloud-host",
  "password": "your-password",
  "port": "443",
  "schema": "YOUR_SCHEMA_NAME",
  "sslValidateCertificate": true,
  "user": "your-username"
}
  • host: The address of your SAP HANA Cloud instance.
  • user and password: The credentials of the user with permissions to access the database schema.
  • schema: The schema where your database objects are located or will be created.
  • encrypt and sslValidateCertificate: Settings to ensure the connection is secure via SSL/TLS.
  • port: The connection port, generally "443" for secure connections.

3: Modify package.json or .cdsrc.json

You need to tell SAP CAP which service to use to obtain the credentials. This is done by modifying your project's package.json or .cdsrc.json file.

Add or update the "requires" section as follows:

{
  "requires": {
    "db": {
      "kind": "hana",
      "credentials": {
        "service": "ups-database-external"
      }
    },
    "[production]": {
      "auth": "xsuaa",
      "db": {
        "kind": "hana",
        "credentials": {
          "service": "ups-database-external"
        }
      },
      "data": false
    }
  }
}
  • "service": "ups-database-external": Indicates to CAP that it should use the provided service named ups-database-external.
  • "[production]": Ensures that the same configuration is used in the production profile.

4: Remove HDI-Related Configurations

Since you are not using HDI for database object management, you need to remove all references and configurations related to HDI in your mta.yaml file or your project's deployment configurations.

This includes:

  • Removing modules or resources that create or manage HDI containers.
  • Deleting dependencies between modules and HDI resources.

5: Bind the Service to the CAP Application Instance in SAP BTP

For your CAP application to take the specified credentials and configuration, you need to bind the provided service to your application's instance in SAP BTP.

You can do this in two ways:

Option 1: Manual Binding

After deploying the application, run the command:

cf bind-service your-application-name ubs-database-external

Then, restart your application:

cf restart your-application-name

Option 2: Automatic Binding in mta.yaml

Add the binding in your mta.yaml file so that it is performed automatically during deployment.

Example of how to define the binding in mta.yaml:

modules:
  - name: your-application-name
    type: nodejs
    path: .
    requires:
      - name: ups-database-external
resources:
  - name: ups-database-external
    type: org.cloudfoundry.managed-service
    parameters:
      service: user-provided
      service-name: ups-database-external

6: Manually Deploy the Database Objects

Since you are not using HDI, you need to manually deploy the database objects in your SAP HANA Cloud instance.

1. Generate the SQL Scripts

From your CDS models, generate the SQL scripts using the following commands:

cds compile db/ --to sql --src db cds compile srv/ --to sql --src srv

These commands generate SQL scripts from your model definitions in the db/ and srv/ directories.

2. Execute the Scripts in the Database Console

  • Access SAP HANA Database Explorer.
  • Connect to your SAP HANA Cloud instance.
  • Execute the generated SQL scripts to create the necessary tables and views in your schema.

Anthony Ramos Romero

entry.title: Using the new selective deletion functionality in SAP DSP to track record deletion in the source
entry.content:
With the latest update of SAP Datasphere, it is now possible to create selective deletions on your local tables created in SAP Datasphere. Obviously, this opens a whole new world of possibilities. One of them is to be able to handle deletions in the source, and even track them in your SAP Datasphere environment. In this series of blogs, I will explain how you could set up such a scenario. In this first blog I will explain how the new functionality for selective deletions on local tables in SAP Datasphere is used.

The new functionality to do selective deletions on your local tables is available from the Data Integration Monitor. From the Local Tables we are able to jump into the settings for a specific local table in SAP Datasphere:

We now get into this new screen:

We are quite easily able to either delete all the records, or selectively delete records from a table based on the value in a column. It is also possible to do this on the values of multiple columns, instead of just one, to really make the deletion specific.

When using the Filter option we have quite a few options to make the correct selection of data that has to be deleted from the local table in SAP Datasphere:

When we have selected the value that we want the deletion to happen on, we can click the Show Preview option to actually show what will be deleted.

This pops up a preview of the data that will be deleted. I expected that this would just be a subset of records, but SAP Datasphere is actually giving a preview of the data that will be deleted, which gives a nice confirmation of what is going to happen.

When clicking on the refresh button in the previous screen we get another confirmation of the number of records that is going to be deleted and the size of the records that will be deleted. With this you could get a nice estimate of the results of your action, and if you need to broaden or narrow your selection to free up more or less space.

Now when we press the button SAP Datasphere will start the deletion of our data based on the criteria.

In the Data Deletion Logs tab, we can keep track of the deletions that previously happened on this table.

We are also able to jump into more detail in what the deletion was by clicking the arrow in window button on the right. Unfortunately, at this moment in time it is not yet possible to see what the delete statement was based on, it will just show you the amount of records deleted.

 

Creating a Data Deletion Schedule

In the same way as manually deleting data you can also set-up a schedule to selectively delete data. We can create a schedule by following the wizard. The first step is to define a business name for the deletion schedule and give the filter values to delete on:

 

The second step is to define a schedule in the same way that we can define a schedule for data loading.

 

Finally in the last step we get an overview of what we scheduled. 

Unfortunately, there is no clear overview over the next deletion that will take place, which I think is something that would add value to have. We can however differentiate the scheduled deletions from the manual deletions because the object type is different:

Another thing that I really miss at this moment in time is the possibility to schedule the selective deletion in a Task Chain. For the use case that we are going to build in the next blog this would be awesome to have.

Conclusion

The new selective deletion functionality in SAP Datasphere provides a powerful way to manage data within local tables. It allows users to selectively remove records based on specific criteria, offering a preview feature to verify what will be deleted. While there are some limitations—such as the lack of a clear overview of scheduled deletions and the inability to add deletions to a Task Chain—this functionality still offers significant control and flexibility.

Particularly for scenarios where data is loaded from external sources like flat files with "full updates," this feature helps manage deletions effectively. By combining it with the power of joins, users can track new, existing, and deleted records, maintaining a clean and up-to-date data environment. Stay tuned for the next blog post, which will explore a practical example of how to leverage this new capability to its fullest potential.

entry.title: Expanding Horizons with our hyperscaler partners and our Multi-Cloud Foundation from SAP-Owned DCs
entry.content:
SAP is thrilled to announce a significant expansion of our datacenter strategy for Business Technology Platform (BTP) strategy.  We are excited to share our progress and unveil additions to our offerings. 


Recent Achievements

We announced our plans in "Expanding Our Horizons: SAP's Build-Out and Datacenter Strategy for SAP BTP" and "Unlock Innovation and Transformation with Expanded SAP BTP and SAP AI," and today we are detailing the expansion of our global presence:

  • Now Live: new datacenter regions in Israel and the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia, leveraging our hyperscaler partners' infrastructure.
  • Coming Soon: By the end of 2024, we'll add new BTP regions in Australia, Japan, and two additional sites in Brazil.
  • Future growth: We're on track to offer at least six additional BTP locations within the next two years, continuing to utilize hyperscaler infrastructure.

We will continue to standardize our service offerings across different datacenters.

A New Chapter: SAP-Owned Datacenters for SAP BTP (Multi-Cloud Foundation)

Today, we're delighted to announce an addition to our datacenter strategy, starting mid-2025, SAP will offer SAP Business Technology Platform (Multi-Cloud Foundation) from SAP-owned datacenters.

  • Coming Soon: In mid-2025 we’ll launch BTP in our first data center in Frankfurt, Germany.
  • Future Growth: As we enter 2026, we aim to have 8 SAP BTP-operated data centers. Our country plans include: Germany, The United Arab Emirates, USA, Japan, China, and Australia.
  • This expansion helps you meet country specific regulations or internal policies for SAP Business Technology Platform operations.
  • Neo migration: Offering SAP BTP (Multi-Cloud foundation) from SAP datacenters also enables existing SAP BTP, Neo Environment customers to migrate to the latest and strategic services of SAP Business Technology Platform without having to change infrastructure provider.

For more information on roadmap and timelines please follow our information on SAP BTP datacenter build-out on SAP Roadmap explorer.

entry.title: SAP HANA Cloud @ TechEd 2024
entry.content:
As usual, SAP TechEd delivers a fireworks show full of innovations for our users. And for sure, SAP HANA Cloud is one of the stars of the show. In this blog, I recap some of the highlights from this year’s event along with the innovations brought to market since TechEd 2023.

 

✨ Key Innovation Announcements

SAP HANA Cloud is our modern Database as a Service (DBaaS) solution. Every quarter, SAP makes this in-memory juggernaut even more powerful. Check out this list of innovations from TechEd 2024. 

 

Intelligent assistance and automation

First up, let’s talk about our intelligent assistant. With the Q4 2024 release, we introduce the concept of database agents. These agents work alongside you to accomplish your day-to-day work tasks as a database administrator or full-stack developer.

Our virtual assistant is designed to streamline database management by offering a range of powerful features. It enables users to execute both single and batch operational tasks through conversational chat, provides guided assistance for complex database tasks, navigates the user interface, and performs quick status analyses of instances. These capabilities help users increase productivity by reducing the time spent on complex tasks and enhance the user experience with faster, more intuitive navigation to relevant applications.

Look forward to automated alert summarization, intelligent issue identification and recommendations meant to drive the optimization of your SAP HANA Cloud landscape.

 

Elastic Compute Nodes for on-demand capacity

A higher memory threshold is only approach of how to handle resource-intensive intelligent solutions. Just as resource requirements increase as an application grows with business demands, so does the need to accommodate more concurrent users and more complex queries (think AI).

We are extremely happy to share that the upcoming Q4 2024 release of SAP HANA Cloud will introduce Elastic Compute Nodes (ECN), a dynamic elasticity feature for optimizing database performance.

An ECN is a read-only compute node that can be provisioned and deprovisioned based on workload demands. Example scenarios that often require additional compute “bandwidth” include heavy planning jobs and real-time stock analysis during Black Friday. The use of an ECN node enables the administrator to accommodate times of heavy usage while maintain the goal of optimizing the SAP HANA database for average use.

ECNs are already used in SAP IBP and SAP Datasphere, to distribute workloads, enhance query performance with temporary tables, and lower TCO by reducing high availability and disaster recovery overhead. Multiple ECNs can be provisioned on demand, offering greater flexibility and efficiency in managing database size.

To get more details about the concept of Elastic Compute Nodes, take a look at this blog post provided by Seungjoon Lee.

 

Lightweight & Native Multitenancy

The upcoming Q4 2024 release of SAP HANA Cloud will introduce native multitenancy at the database level. Soon, customers can manage data in separate database tenants within the same SAP HANA Cloud, SAP HANA database instance.
This multitenant model improves cost-effectiveness by sharing resources while maintaining logical data separation. Each database tenant will be encrypted with customer-specific keys, offering individual tenant-level administration and lifecycle management capabilities (incl. backup & recovery, move, etc.)
Additional features, such as workload management to prevent resource contention, tenant data copying, and online tenant moves, will provide scalability and flexibility in future.
 
With this capability of deploying multiple application instances to a single SAP HANA Cloud database, developers can now build SaaS-like applications. Customers and partners can easily deliver a public-cloud model for their applications while efficiently using resources and providing enhanced security. 

To learn more about multitenancy, take a look at this blog post provided by Product Expert Rüdiger Karl.

 

Knowledge Graph Engine

Let's talk about another announcement that we're proudly sharing:

In Q1 2025, we will introduce the knowledge graph engine as part of SAP HANA Cloud, offering 

businesses a powerful tool to connect and analyze data through semantically connected relationships.
This upcoming addition to SAP HANA Cloud's multi-model data processing engines will allow for smarter applications by using data stored in knowledge graphs to answer complex questions, enhancing large language models (LLMs) and generative AI capabilities.

Key features include native RDF triple store support, interoperability between SQL and SPARQL, and the ability to create a unified view of data, from scattered sources. With the new knowledge graph engine being present, our users will benefit from improved decision-making support, enhanced AI use cases, and the establishment of data fabrics for holistic data analysis.

In this blog post my dear colleague Shabana Samsudheen is giving more details about the capability and it's power and relevance for you as an SAP BTP developer. Don't miss this must-read!

 

Generative AI toolkit for SAP HANA Cloud

During the executive keynote, Michael Ameling introduced the new Generative AI toolkit for SAP HANA Cloud (available Q1 2025).

Integrated into SAP Build Code, the toolkit enables SAP BTP developers to implement their business scenarios using Intelligent Data Applications. Thanks to the natural language interface, developers will find the process to create in-database machine learning models very easy.

For instance, including advanced forecasts and adding additional insights to existing applications often required multi-team collaboration. Thus, the toolkit’s use of automatic code generation not only helps casual data users to benefit from SAP HANA's native ML capabilities, but also instructs users how to apply the powerful features themselves. 

 

✨Innovation Highlights introduced since last years SAP TechEd

The innovations announced at TechEd 2024 only build on a year of success for SAP HANA Cloud. There are three major innovation delivered in 2024 that should be added to this notable list of highlights: the SAP HANA Cloud vector engine, the consumption monitor, and SQL on files. 

Vector Engine & Similarity Search

Introduced in Q1 2024, the vector engine represents a powerful addition to SAP HANA Cloud's multi-model data processing and AI capabilities. The vector engine can efficiently store and process vector embeddings as well as perform fully in-memory similarity searches. SAP HANA Cloud now is now the go-to database for augmenting generative AI scenarios. 

Large Language Models are very helpful tools for a large variety of use cases. Even though they rely on a broad-spectrum of knowledge, these models regularly lack relevant business context. LLM’s do not have access to sensitive data often needed for business specific responses needed by generative AI use cases. SAP HANA Cloud’s vector engine, in conjunction with SAP's Generative AI Hub, work together to mitigate these challenges and add significant value to the user’s experience. 

For more information on the SAP HANA Cloud vector engine, take a look at this blog post provided by product expert Shabana Samsudheen.  

 

Advanced cost transparency & financial governance

In Q2 2024 the so-called "Consumption Monitor" was introduced into SAP HANA Cloud Central - giving customers easier insights into capacity unit consumption by SAP HANA Cloud instances. 

This feature is now part of the administration and monitoring tool and provides historical, estimated, and actual values for memory, compute, storage, and network data transfer. The monitor simplifies access to consumption data by offering clear insights and simple forecasting.

SAP HANA Cloud customers can use the reporting to optimize resource use and control costs. A better understanding of resource consumption and associated spending can provide the data needed to manage the overall total cost of ownership and optimize costs in-line with the business requirements. 

 

File-based query processing using SQL on files

In today's data-driven world, enterprises collect vast amounts of data. Unlocking valuable insights from this data while connecting SAP and non-SAP applications is critical for staying competitive.

Starting with SAP HANA Cloud QRC 03/2024, SAP HANA Database introduces native SQL on Files, enabling direct access to data in common formats like CSV, P

arquet, and Delta tables (Open Table Format). This allows users to run powerful analytical queries on structured data without moving or loading it into the database, improving cost-performance balance and flexibility.

Key features include one-click linking to SAP HANA Cloud data lake Files, creating read-only virtual tables, and support for file formats like CSV, Parquet, and Delta tables. Additionally, Delta tables support time travel queries, allowing users to query data at specific versions or timestamps.

For more information on file-based architectures with SAP HANA Cloud, take a look at this blog post by my esteem colleague Seungjoon Lee.

✨Session Replays

In case you have missed any TechEd 2024 sessions live, don't worry! You can meanwhile find the replays for all sessions online; just navigate to your content of interest via the session catalog.

Here, you can find the replays for all sessions related to SAP HANA Cloud:

DA100 | Data in the age of AI
DA101 | SAP HANA Cloud: Recent innovations, road map, and strategy
DA102 | AI-enabled clean core with SAP HANA Cloud
DA103 | Generative AI–driven business transformation enabled by SAP HANA Cloud
DA181 | Build AI-enabled intelligent data applications to help keep the core clean
 

✨ Stay connected with SAP HANA Cloud and the product team

Make sure to follow the SAP HANA Cloud tag for updates on all the latest developments. Check out the whatsnewinsaphanacloud tag for new blog posts. To learn more about recently introduced innovations, or don't want to miss any new webcasts, pay close attention to this YouTube playlist.

Finally, be sure to sign-up for the SAP HANA Cloud Community. The site is a great place to post questions about topics covered at SAP TechEd 2024, to learn more about SAP HANA Cloud in general, or even to discuss any of the innovations mentioned in this blog. 

This year for SAP HANA Cloud is full of great innovations and next year will be just as exciting. Thank you for trusting SAP HANA Cloud with your data and most critical business logic. We can’t wait to hear about what you will do with these great innovations.

entry.title: 🚀Fire of Innovations: Highlights from SAP TechEd 2024 📢
entry.content:
The SAP TechEd 2024 event, themed “Fire of Innovations,” has once again set the stage for groundbreaking advancements in enterprise technology. Held virtually on October 8-9, this year’s conference showcased a plethora of new tools, updates, and strategies designed to empower businesses and developers alike. Here’s a deep dive into some of the most exciting announcements and innovations from the event.

1. Joule: The AI Copilot Revolution

One of the standout highlights was the unveiling of new capabilities for Joule, SAP’s generative AI copilot. Joule has been supercharged with collaborative AI agents that can perform complex, cross-disciplinary tasks. These agents are designed to break down silos within organizations, enhancing productivity and allowing human workers to focus on more creative and strategic activities.

For instance, in dispute management, autonomous AI agents can now analyze and resolve issues such as incorrect invoices or duplicate payments. In financial accounting, these agents streamline processes like bill payments and ledger updates, quickly addressing any inconsistencies.

2. SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) Enhancements

SAP continues to expand its Business Technology Platform (BTP), which is central to its cloud strategy. This year, SAP announced the expansion of BTP coverage through eight new SAP-managed data centers. Additionally, the SAP Discovery Center now offers a Quick-Account-Setup feature, enabling businesses to deploy specific use cases with just a click.

The integration of ABAP Cloud into SAP Build was another significant update. This integration allows developers to create and maintain ABAP Cloud projects directly from the SAP Build lobby, ensuring a seamless and efficient development process.

3. Innovations in SAP Build

SAP Build has been strengthened to support a “Clean Core” strategy, which is crucial for maintaining system consistency and upgradeability. The SAP Build Extensibility Wizard, introduced for SAP S/4HANA Cloud, Public Edition, helps both key users and developers create on-stack and side-by-side extensions in an embedded experience.

4. Harnessing the Power of Data with SAP Knowledge Graph

SAP introduced the SAP Knowledge Graph, a next-generation solution that connects SAP data with rich business context. This tool is poised to help developers unlock the full value of SAP data, driving more informed decision-making and innovation.

5. Global Reach and Accessibility

This year’s SAP TechEd was a truly global event, with a free two-day virtual format that included keynotes, technical sessions, and developer enablement activities. The event was designed to be accessible to a worldwide audience, ensuring that everyone could benefit from the latest SAP innovations.

Conclusion

SAP TechEd 2024 has once again demonstrated SAP’s commitment to driving innovation and empowering businesses with cutting-edge technology. From AI advancements with Joule to enhancements in SAP BTP and SAP Build, the “Fire of Innovations” theme truly encapsulates the transformative potential of these new tools and strategies. As businesses continue to navigate the complexities of the digital age, SAP’s innovations provide a robust foundation for growth and success.

Stay tuned for more updates and insights from SAP TechEd as we continue to explore the future of enterprise technology.

Feel free to share your thoughts or ask any questions about the event! What innovation are you most excited about?

entry.title: SAP TechEd 2024 - Summary
entry.content:
SAP TechEd 2024 delivered an inspiring keynote with a strong focus on how technology drives real-world business innovations. This session brought to light the latest advances in AI, cloud solutions, and SAP's vision for the future of enterprise software.

The keynote opened with a thought-provoking introduction about the human brain, its complexities, and how innovation emerges from millions of connections forming new ideas. This metaphor seamlessly tied into SAP's philosophy of turning ideas into tangible solutions—where concepts become tools for solving real business problems.

Innovation at the Heart of SAP’s Strategy

One of the central themes was the growing importance of Artificial Intelligence (AI) in SAP’s offerings. AI is now deeply integrated into SAP’s cloud-driven portfolio, designed to simplify customers' lives and meet evolving business needs. Senior AI Scientists at SAP are working on harnessing AI to make business software smarter and more intuitive. The keynote also emphasized SAP's efforts to ensure that its software is configurable, extensible, and unified, providing a seamless suite-like experience across its platforms.

SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP)

A significant highlight was SAP's Business Technology Platform (BTP), which has seen rapid growth in customer adoption. BTP is the cornerstone for SAP’s cloud and AI-driven future, empowering businesses with application development, automation, data analytics, and AI capabilities all within one unified platform. The keynote highlighted that over 27,000 live customers and 2,800 partners are leveraging SAP BTP to drive innovation.

The announcement of new BTP regions running on infrastructure from partners like AWS, Microsoft, and Google, along with SAP-managed data centers, shows SAP’s commitment to providing broader regional coverage and reliable service. This expansion aims to help businesses operate globally while maintaining efficiency, security, and compliance.

Enhancements in Application Development and Automation

SAP unveiled its Clean Core Strategy, which allows businesses to build custom extensions in a way that keeps the core ERP system maintainable and upgradeable. With SAP’s low-code environment, developers can create AI-powered applications with ease, while ABAP Cloud now supports both on-stack and side-by-side development, giving businesses more flexibility in their customizations.

One exciting feature introduced was the SAP Build Extensibility Wizard, a tool designed to streamline the process of creating custom fields and extensions, allowing users to enhance SAP applications with minimal coding.

SAP Build and GenAI

In keeping with the AI theme, SAP showcased the GenAI capabilities within SAP Build. With Joule, an AI assistant, developers can create complex data models, build extensions, and generate applications through natural language prompts. This AI-driven approach reduces manual coding and helps users integrate advanced functionalities like time series forecasting and predictive analytics into their business processes.

Data and Analytics Innovations

The keynote further highlighted innovations in data and analytics, specifically focusing on SAP HANA Cloud, SAP Analytics Cloud, and SAP Datasphere. The HANA Cloud Vector Engine and Knowledge Graph Engine were introduced, both of which enable businesses to harness structured and unstructured data for more context-aware AI-driven decisions. These engines promise faster and more accurate data processing, particularly in scenarios involving large language models and retrieval-augmented generation (RAG).

Additionally, the keynote showcased how SAP Datasphere is being adopted by large enterprises like Hershey’s and Bosch for data cataloging, integration, and self-service analytics, empowering businesses to access trusted data with ease.

Integration Suite and AI-Driven Optimization

The SAP Integration Suite now offers a modern, AI-powered solution for real-time end-to-end business process integration. With thousands of pre-built integrations and connectors, businesses can seamlessly integrate SAP and third-party applications. SAP is also leveraging AI-generated recommendations to optimize custom scripts, further enhancing developer productivity and reducing memory consumption.

Conclusion

SAP TechEd 2024 demonstrated SAP’s unwavering commitment to making innovation accessible, impactful, and transformative. From the integration of advanced AI capabilities to the expansion of BTP, SAP is setting a new standard in enterprise software. Whether you are looking to enhance your application development, automate processes, or harness the power of data, SAP’s latest innovations offer powerful tools to bring your ideas to life.

As SAP continues to evolve, the future of enterprise technology is more connected, intelligent, and adaptable than ever before.

Here is the Keynote from SAP:

Comment below your thoughts on TechEd.

entry.title: Configuring OAuth 2.0 between SAP C4C and SAP BTP
entry.content:
You need to create HTTP destinations in the SAP BTP cockpit. These destinations provide data communication via HTTP protocol.

You can use the SAML Bearer assertion flow for consuming OAuth-protected resources. Users are authenticated by using SAML against the configured trusted identity providers. The SAML assertion is then used to request an access token from an OAuth authorization server. This access token must be added as an Authorization header with value Bearer <access token> in all HTTP requests to the OAuth-protected resources.

WORKFLOW

  1. Configure OAuth Identity Provider in SAP Cloud for Customer.
  2. Configure the OAuth Client for OData Access to SAP Cloud for Customer OData APIs.
  3. Create and Configure the HTTP Destination.

1. Configure OAuth Identity Provider in SAP Cloud for Customer:

  1. Log on to the SAP BTP cockpit, and configure the settings as below,
    1. Navigate to your extension subaccount in the Cloud Foundry environment.
    2. Choose Connectivity ->Destination Trust.
    3. Choose Export to get the active trust certificate for this subaccount and save it on your local file system.                                                              
    4. Open the certificate in a text editor, copy the content between -----BEGIN CERTIFICATE----- and -----END CERTIFICATE-----, and save it in a file with the following format <subaccount>_signing.cer, where <subaccount> is the Subaccount Name from the Subaccount Information of your subaccount.
  2. Log on to your SAP Cloud for Customer system as an administrator. Go to ADMINISTRATOR ->Common Tasks.
  3. Choose Configure OAuth 2.0 Identity Provider ->New OAuth2.0 Provider, and configure the settings as below,                                                                                                                                            
    1. In the Issuing Entity Name field, enter cfapps.<region_host>/<subaccountID>. You go to the SAP BTP cockpit, navigate to the subaccount, go to Overview and,
      • For the <region_host>, copy the API endpoint from the Cloud Foundry section, and remove the https://api.cf..
      • For the <subaccountID>, copy the ID from the Subaccount Details section.
      • For example: cfapps.eu10.hana.ondemand.com/12345678-1234-1234-1234-1234578.
    2. From the Primary Signing Certificate field, browse the <subaccount>_signing.cer file that you saved.
    3. Select the E-Mail Address checkbox.
    4. Choose Submit.

Here is an example for OAuth 2.0 Identity Provider:                                 

2. Configure the OAuth Client for OData Access to SAP Cloud for Customer OData APIs:

  1. Log on to your SAP Cloud for Customer system as an administrator.
  2. Go to ADMINISTRATOR ->OAUTH2.0 CLIENT REGISTRATION.
  3. Choose New,                                                        
  4. Specify a client secret, description, and token lifetime (in seconds). For example, 3600 seconds.
  5. In the Issuer Name field, use the dropdown list to specify the identity provider that you created.
  6. Copy the entry in the Client ID field. You will need it later when creating the HTTP destination with OAuth authentication required for the connectivity to the SAP Cloud for Customer OData APIs.                                     
  7. In the Scope list, select the UIWC:CC_HOME scope.
  8. Choose Save and Close.                                            

3. Create and Configure the HTTP Destination:

  1. Log on to your SAP BTP cockpit, navigate to your subaccount.
  2. Choose Connectivity ->Destinations in the navigation panel.
  3. Create an HTTP destination.
  4. To enable principal propagation, create an OAuth2SAMLBearerAssertion HTTP destination,                                     
    1. Configure the basic settings:                                                                     
      ParameterValue
      NameEnter a meaningful name.
      TypeHTTP
      Description(Optional) Enter a meaningful description.
      URLhttps://<my_SAP_Cloud_for_Customer_system_name>.crm.ondemand.com/sap/c4c/odata/v1/c4codataapi
      Proxy TypeInternet
      AuthenticationOAuth2SAMLBearerAssertion
      AudienceGo to the SAP Cloud for Customer administration view, then navigate to Configure Single Sign-On under General Settings and copy the value from the Local Service Provider field.
      Client KeyClient ID
      Paste the entry you have copied from the Client ID field when configuring the OAuth client.
      Token Service URLhttps://<my_SAP_Cloud_for_Customer_system_name>.crm.ondemand.com/sap/bc/sec/oauth2/token
      Token Service UserClient ID
      Paste the entry you have copied from the Client ID field when configuring the OAuth client.
      Token Service PasswordClient secret
      Paste the entry you have copied from the Client Secret field when configuring the OAuth client.
    2. Configure the required additional property. To do so, in the Additional Properties panel, choose New Property, and enter the following property:                                                                                   
      ParameterValue
      scopeScope ID entries separated by space.
      Paste the entry you have copied from the Client ID field when configuring the OAuth client.
      Example: UIWC:CC_HOME
    3. Select the Use default JDK truststore checkbox.
    4. Save your entries.

Here is an example for a destination definition and additional parameters:

In this way, we can configure the OAuth 2.0 between SAP Cloud for Customer and SAP Business Technology Platform.

Thanks,
Arun

entry.title: LEVEREDGE 2024 Highlights: Discovering New Strategies for Digital Transformation
entry.content:
Organizations face a multitude of challenges today, from adapting to rapid technological advancements to navigating complex regulatory environments. LEVEREDGE 2024 was created to address these pressing issues and to provide a platform where industry leaders come together to explore the latest strategies, technologies, and solutions shaping the future of business.

Over three days, this premier event gathered 400+ guests and thought leaders from 10+ countries to share insights, experiences, and success stories. Attendees had the opportunity to engage in diverse sessions, including keynote presentations, panel discussions, and interactive workshops, covering vital topics such as Business AI, Digital Supply Chain, Intelligent Spend & Procurement, Data Migration, S/4HANA, Product Lifecycle Management, and much more.

Key Highlights of Day 1

The event kicked off with a Design Thinking Networking Breakfast, setting the tone for meaningful connections among participants. This engaging atmosphere fostered discussions that sparked new ideas and collaborative solutions.

The day continued with Design Thinking Workshops led by the LeverX AppHaus Team and Andreas Hauser, Senior Vice President of the SAP AppHaus Network, where participants explored AI-driven solutions.

Further collaborative discussions emphasized the potential of AI and fostered a spirit of collaboration, encouraging attendees to address the challenges businesses face on their digital transformation journeys.

Key Highlights of Day 2

Day 2 of LEVEREDGE 2024 was all about deepening our understanding of the latest advancements in digital transformation, artificial intelligence, and innovative business practices.

Spanning three dynamic stages — the Blue Stage, Green Stage, and the Yellow Stage (SAP PLM Forum) — the agenda featured keynotes from industry leaders, expert panels, and interactive discussions, each offering valuable perspectives on the evolving role of technology in today’s business landscape. From AI-powered solutions to groundbreaking PLM innovations, the day was packed with insightful conversations, practical takeaways, and plenty of opportunities to network with peers and thought leaders.

 

🔵 Blue Stage highlights

Opening remarks: Motivational speeches from Dr. Victor Lozinski, Co-Founder and Chairman of the Board at LeverX, and Daniella Levine Cava, Mayor of Miami-Dade County.

  • Maximizing business efficiency: How leveraging SAP technology can unlock new levels of operational efficiency and enhance customer experiences.
  • AI in research & development: The role of AI in accelerating research and development processes, particularly in the pharmaceutical sector, driving faster innovation.
  • AI & digitalization for business sustainability: An exploration of how AI and digitalization are transforming business models, driving sustainability, and supporting growth in various industries.
  • Data modernization: The critical importance of modernizing data strategies to maintain a competitive edge in today’s rapidly evolving market.
  • Innovative service solutions: Multi-platform service applications that deliver enhanced operational capabilities and customer value were showcased.
  • Leadership & technology: How effective leadership can help organizations navigate technological advancements and leverage them for business growth.

🟢 Green Stage highlights

  • AI-powered Clean Core extensions: How SAP Business Technology Platform enhances key extensions with AI to drive innovation and operational efficiency.
  • Cybersecurity and AI: An exploration of the collaborative role of AI and Governance, Risk, and Compliance (GRC) solutions in improving SAP cybersecurity strategies.
  • Generative AI in enterprise operations: The benefits of integrating generative AI solutions from cloud platforms like AWS to transform enterprise operations.
  • ERP transformation success: A case study on the successful implementation of S/4HANA to streamline ERP transformation in the manufacturing sector.
  • Warehousing innovations: Emerging trends and innovations in SAP warehousing solutions that optimize supply chain processes.
  • Enhanced employee experiences: The discussion on how mobile technology, AI, and user-centered design to improve employee engagement and experiences.
  • HR transformation with SAP SuccessFactors: The journey of implementing SAP SuccessFactors to drive operational excellence in human resource management.
  • Digital transformation in the food industry: A real-world example of digital transformation within the food and beverage industry, focusing on improving operational efficiency and customer experience.

🟡Yellow Stage highlights

  • SAP PLM vision & strategy: An exploration of the latest developments and future strategies for SAP Product Lifecycle Management (PLM), focusing on innovation and business growth.
  • Global PLM implementation success: A real-world case study demonstrating the successful implementation of a global PLM system in discrete industries, highlighting its transformative effects on product lifecycle management.
  • Panel on PLM trends and challenges: A discussion of key trends in digital twins, product design, and the critical role of integrating PLM into enterprise resource planning (ERP) systems to drive business efficiency.

Breakout sessions:

  • Specification management & formulation: A deep dive into process PLM, focusing on the complexities of managing product specifications and formulation processes.
  • Integrating digital twins in PLM and ERP: An exploration of how digital twins are becoming an essential component of product design and how they integrate with SAP's PLM and ERP solutions.

 

As Day 2 of LEVEREDGE 2024 came to a close, a clear theme emerged: the critical role that AI, digital transformation, and innovative leadership play in shaping the future of business. As industries continue to innovate, the integration of AI, data-driven solutions, and leadership will remain key drivers of sustainable growth and transformation.

Key Highlights of Day 3

Day 3 of LEVEREDGE 2024 was all about embracing the transformative power of AI, digital transformation, and leadership innovation. As the final day of the event unfolded, two primary stages — the Blue Stage and the Yellow Stage (SAP PLM Forum) — hosted a range of insightful sessions. 

🔵 Blue Stage highlights

  • AI for business efficiency: Introduction to the practical integration of AI into business operations, focusing on enhancing efficiency and scalability.
  • AI in logistics: Insights on how AI is transforming logistics, improving delivery precision, and optimizing supply chain management.
  • Leadership and innovation: A session exploring the intersection of leadership and technology, offering lessons from leadership journeys and innovative business practices.
  • Digital transformation case studies: Real-world examples of how businesses are leveraging AI, mobile technology, and SAP solutions to drive digital transformation and operational excellence.
  • AWS and SAP BTP for innovation: Discussion on how AWS and SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) are enabling organizations to innovate and streamline processes for better business outcomes.
  • Mobile work order management: A demonstration of the technology behind mobile work order management, showcasing its ability to simplify workflows and enhance operational efficiency.

AI-powered solutions in action: Use cases illustrating how AI-powered solutions are driving industry transformation, with examples of businesses adopting AI to enhance their operations.

  • Digital transformation in transportation: Insights into how companies are using SAP S/4HANA and Transportation Management to achieve operational efficiencies in the transportation sector.
  • Maximizing SAP investments: An exploration of the strategic benefits of engaging with user groups to fully unlock the potential of SAP investments.
  • Managing vendor and supply chain risks: Best practices for navigating risks in vendor and supply chain management, with a focus on procurement and mitigation strategies.

🟡 Yellow Stage (SAP PLM Forum) highlights

  • Sustainable innovation: An exploration of how PLM drives innovation while ensuring sustainability and compliance.
  • AI in product development: How AI improves the efficiency of new product development processes.
  • PLM success stories: Real-world examples of PLM implementations, showcasing business transformation.

PLM security: Best practices for securing PLM applications and protecting data.

  • Product data governance: Managing product data and engineering changes effectively.
  • Industry-specific roadmaps: Future advancements in PLM for discrete and process industries.

As Day 3 concluded, a key takeaway was the undeniable impact of AI and digital transformation on industries across the board. From revolutionizing logistics and supply chains to driving sustainable innovation through PLM solutions, the sessions underscored how technology is reshaping modern business practices. The importance of leadership, collaboration, and continuous learning was evident, as attendees left with fresh perspectives and strategies to implement in their own organizations. LEVEREDGE 2024 concluded with a clear message: innovation, driven by AI and digital tools, is the future of business success.

The Bottom Line

LEVEREDGE 2024 showcased the future of business, emphasizing the pivotal role of AI, digital transformation, and leadership in driving innovation. Across three days of insightful sessions, industry leaders and experts shared their visions and practical strategies for navigating the evolving business landscape. From AI-powered logistics to sustainable PLM solutions, the event highlighted how technology is reshaping industries. As attendees leave equipped with fresh perspectives, the key takeaway is clear: embracing innovation and leveraging digital tools are essential for achieving lasting business success.

entry.title: Sustainability in Defense and Security: Why It’s Essential
entry.content:
Why Sustainability Matters

 

“We simply must do everything we can in our power to slow down global warming before it is too late. We can save our planet and also boost our economy at the same time.”

 

You may now be wondering what this has to do with Defense and Security organizations such as armed forces, firefighters, police, ambulance services, or aid organizations. The answer is simply more than most people think. For one thing, according to the leading consultancy McKinsey, D&S organizations account for approximately 50% of governments’ carbon emissions from fleets and facilities in countries like Canada and the United Kingdom, and up to about 80% in the United States of America. On the other hand, if you consider global CO2 emissions, the military sector accounts for 1-5%. Although this stake does not seem high at first glance, it certainly does when you set it in relation to other business sectors and see that the emissions are comparably high as in the aviation or shipping industry. This means that any government that aims to reduce its overall emissions needs to consider its defense forces in its climate action plan too. For another thing, current, state-of-the-art sustainability solutions do not really harm the organizational performance or the practical operational readiness of military organizations anymore. This is especially because key processes, such as the measurement of emissions, are usually run in the background through automated calculations and the power of highly innovative sensor technology and software. But more on that later. Before that, I want to inform you about some additional statistical data and facts about sustainability. The consulting company Boston Consulting Group (BCG) found that the global defense industry’s share in global CO2 emissions could rise significantly from 2% in 2021 to 25% in 2050 if the industry does not take active action to reduce its emissions. But what do these emissions look like in detail?

 

Figure 1: CO2 Emissions of the US Department of Defense (2023)

 

As the above pie chart of the CO2 emissions of the US Department of Defense in 2023 shows, most emissions result from vehicles and equipment or purchased electricity. In Germany, however, where the emissions of the Bundeswehr increased by 17.9% in the period of 2019 to 2021, we see a somewhat different picture since most of the increase results from increased fuel consumption of the air force as well as increased heating requirements of the Bundeswehr’s military infrastructure. Interestingly, as the well-known Nature magazine pointed out in an article, many military organizations around the world still do not measure and report their emissions in as much detail as other governmental organizations or private companies do. In contrast, 95% of the largest companies globally publish ESG (environmental, social, and governance) reports regularly.

 

What Actions are Required to meet the Climate Targets?

 

However, although the above statistics may not look really promising to the reader, we luckily see that the problem was recognized a long time ago by many Defense & Security organizations. Military organizations such as the US Department of Defense, the German Bundeswehr, or NATO have actively created climate action plans within the last few years:

 

The North Atlantic Treaty Organization (NATO) in this regard set itself the following targets in 2021:

  • Reduction of greenhouse gas emissions by at least 45% by 2030.
  • Achievement of net zero emissions (Net zero emissions occur when the amount of greenhouse gases emitted equals the amount removed from the atmosphere) by 2050.

 

The European Union, on the other hand, has set the defense sector within the Union similar climate action goals:

  • Reduction of greenhouse gas emissions by at least 55% by 2030 compared to 1990 levels.
  • Carbon neutrality by 2050.
  • Increased use of renewable energies and focus on improvement of energy efficiency within military operations.
  • Implementation of sustainable practices in defense operations.
  • Passing of a so-called “Climate-Security Nexus”, that addresses the link between climate change and security threats, thereby including climate considerations into defense planning and operations.

 

As can be seen in the text above, there are already a number of promising strategic plans, that include proven concepts and best practices for reducing greenhouse gas emissions of military organizations. However, specialized management consultancies such as BCG and McKinsey in t his regard have drawn up a number of additional recommendations for making defense organizations more sustainable:

 

Recommendations from McKinsey

McKinsey suggests leveraging low-carbon technologies by using electric vehicles, sustainable aviation fuel, and renewable energy sources. Additionally, the consultancy recommends putting the focus mainly on non-mission-critical areas. This according to McKinsey allows armed forces organizations to achieve a 60% reduction in Scope 1 and 2 emissions. Other suggestions from the company include to establish an end-to-end consideration of sustainability in value chains and supply chains of Defense and Security organizations.

 

Recommendations from the Boston Consulting Group:

The US-based consulting company Boston Consulting Company (BCG) principally makes similar suggestions. It’s main recommendation is to make significant investments into sustainable technologies through research and development. Furthermore, it highly recommends D&S organizations to enhance emissions reporting and accountability as well as to pursue net-zero emission goals.

 

Other Recommendations and Initiatives:

The consulting company KPMG suggests D&S organizations to align their climate goals and actions with international and national climate targets, whereas Nature advocates for mandatory emissions reporting and international agreements and methodologies to monitor and reduce military emissions. Additionally, D&S organizations such as the US DoD or the US Army have established their own guidelines for water efficiency and water security.

 

Sustainability Targets of Emergency Organizations:

These recommendations are not only true for military organizations, but are applicable to emergency organizations like Police, Ambulances or Firefighting organizations in a similar manner. Examples include organizations such as the London Fire Brigade, which has outlined a “Sustainable Development Strategy for 2023-2025” and consequently invested in measures like acquiring electric vehicles and making their fire stations more energy efficient. Other examples in this direction include the Berlin Police, that has implemented measures such as using hybrid and electric cars along with sustainable practices such as a focus on recycling and a reduction of paper use. In doing so the emergency organization aims to support the City of Berlin in reaching its climate reduction 2030 and climate neutrality 2045 goals.

 

Given the above-mentioned challenges and facts, I now want to draw the reader’s attention to the holistic SAP Sustainability Solutions, that allow D&S organizations to actively tackle and manage all common aspects of sustainability.  

 

The SAP Sustainability Solutions

 

 

Video 1: ERP to ESG: How to develop your first ESG Strategy

 

SAP is proud to be one of the leading software suppliers for Enterprise Resource Planning and other software solutions for various leading companies and organizations around the world. However, what is less well-known across customers and industries is that SAP also offers powerful and holistic cross-industry sustainability software solutions. The leading technology research companies, Gartner and IDC, have recognized SAP’s sustainability solutions for their comprehensive capabilities. Gartner has rated SAP as a preferred vendor for carbon accounting and management software solutions, while IDC has praised the “breadth and depth” of SAP’s offerings. The SAP solutions are thereby not only highly capable of measuring, analyzing, and managing carbon emissions, but are also trusted by leading companies and organizations worldwide, such as IBM, PWC, Freudenberg, msg global, and SAP itself. SAP’s sustainability solutions feature real-time data analytics, seamless integration with existing ERP systems, and AI-driven insights to help businesses make informed decisions. For example, IBM has leveraged SAP’s solutions to significantly reduce its carbon footprint, while PWC has used them to enhance its sustainability reporting. SAP’s commitment to sustainability has also been recognized with several industry awards, and the company continues to innovate with new features and developments on the horizon.

In general, the SAP Sustainability Cloud software suite is built on the following four main software solutions:

 

1) Sustainability and ESG Reporting Software 

 

Video 2: SAP Sustainability Control Tower – Holistic Steering and ESG Reporting

 

Figure 2: The SAP Sustainability Control Tower solution

 

SAP allows companies and organizations to connect their environmental, social, and governance (ESG) data and steer their organizations towards more sustainable and efficient operations, thereby also helping to achieve national and international climate goals. The SAP ESG software was designed in a holistic way and provides streamlined reporting as well as valuable insights for decision-makers and stakeholders. Furthermore, it allows for the full integration of the organization’s stakeholders, like suppliers, into the system. This also means that governments theoretically can implement the solution nation-wide or across different governmental areas for managing everything around sustainability with one central software platform. Apart from that, the software generally allows D&S organizations to measure, communicate, and achieve corporate sustainability goals to the broader public. Moreover, the sustainability software is integrable across various different systems, even across the respective organization or company. This allows bringing sustainability, safety, risk, compliance, asset, and people data together in one place for enabling comprehensive sustainability reporting and analysis.

 

2) SAP Climate Action Solutions

 

Video 3: Transactional Carbon Accounting with SAP

 

Figure 3: Measuring emissions with the SAP Sustainability Management solutions

 

The SAP Climate Action Solutions allow companies and organizations to decarbonize their entire value chain from end-to-end to become climate-neutral organizations. This is made possible by integrating actual business transactions with carbon accounting software, emissions and compliance management, carbon footprint calculations, e-mobility, and transportation logistics. The solution includes services like:

  • Real-time decision making with SAP Sustainability Footprint Management
  • SAP Sustainability Data Exchange
  • SAP Environment, Health and Safety Management
  • and many more services

 

3) Circular Economy Software 

 

Video 4: Circular Design with SAP

 

Figure 4: The SAP Circular Economy solutions

 

The SAP Circular Economy solutions allow companies and organizations to integrate the concept of circularity into their organization. Circularity thereby allows them to reduce the use of resources and materials, such as plastic, as much as possible. In addition, the concept aims to eliminate waste and circulate materials. Our Circular Economy solutions thereby consider all these requirements and allow managing circularity holistically. This includes circularity in supply chains, production, and all other applicable organizational processes.
